1: A man of God (ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm, H376 + H430) came from Judah to Bethel by the word of YHWH (bĕḏĕḇar YHWH) — the introductory phrase establishes both the messenger’s authority (ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm) and his commission (bĕḏĕḇar YHWH, by the command/word of YHWH). His southern origin — from Judah, the territory of the Davidic covenant — makes his appearance at Jeroboam’s altar in Bethel geographically and theologically charged. Jeroboam was standing beside the altar (ʿōmēd ʿal-hammizḇēaḥ) to offer incense — the king assuming priestly functions, combining the royal and sacerdotal offices that the covenant carefully separated.
2: wayyiqrāʾ ʿal-hammizḇēaḥ bĕḏĕḇar YHWH wayyōʾmar mizḇēaḥ mizḇēaḥ kōh-ʾāmar YHWH — He cried out against the altar by the word of YHWH: “O altar, altar, thus says YHWH.” The apostrophe to the altar itself — the direct address of an inanimate object with the divine oracle — is unusual and rhetorically powerful. The oracle names Josiah (yōšîyāhû) by name as the future destroyer of the altar, approximately 300 years before his birth and reign (2 Kings 23:15-20). This is one of the most explicit predictive prophecies in the Hebrew Bible — a specific personal name given centuries in advance. The priest’s bones burned on the altar is the image of ultimate desecration: not mere destruction but the use of the altar’s own sacred fire to consume those who served at it.
3: ûmôpēṯ nāṯan bayyôm hahûʾ — he gave a sign (môpēṯ, H4159) on that same day. môpēṯ is the sign-wonder that authenticates the prophet’s word (cf. Deuteronomy 13:1-3 where signs and wonders must be tested against doctrinal fidelity; Exodus 4:21 where môpēṯ refers to the plague miracles). The sign given is the immediate splitting of the altar and spilling of the ashes — a visible confirmation of the long-term prediction. In prophetic epistemology (Deuteronomy 18:22), the fulfillment of the near-term prediction authenticates the distant one. The altar splitting validates the Josiah prophecy by demonstrating YHWH’s power to immediately act on his declared word.
4: Jeroboam stretched out his hand from the altar — šallaḥ yāḏô mēʿal hammizḇēaḥ — and said “seize him!” (tiśpĕsûhû). The attempt to arrest the man of God is the direct human countermeasure to the divine word — the king’s authority challenged by prophetic authority, with the king asserting royal power. The hand that stretched out to command the arrest immediately withered: yibbāš yāḏô (his hand dried up/withered). yāḇēš (H3001, to dry up/wither) is the term for plant withering in drought — the hand becomes a shriveled, powerless thing. The bodily sign confirms the prophetic authority over royal power.
5: The altar split apart and the ashes poured from it, according to the môpēṯ the man of God had given by the word of YHWH. The simultaneous occurrence of both signs — the withered hand and the splitting altar — in direct response to the royal intervention against the prophet validates YHWH’s sovereign protection of his messenger. The altar’s destruction begins immediately even as the distant fulfillment (the Josiah prediction) remains centuries away. The proximate signs are YHWH’s witnesses to the reliability of his distant word.
6: ḥal-nā ʾeṯ-pĕnê YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā — “Please seek the face of YHWH your God.” Jeroboam’s address — “YHWH your God” (not “our God”) — reveals that the king recognizes YHWH’s authority in this moment while distancing himself from personal covenant relationship. The man of God prays; YHWH restores the king’s hand. The restoration of the withered hand despite the king’s attempt to arrest YHWH’s messenger is YHWH’s gracious response to prayer even from the agent of a rebellious king. The grace is real, but Jeroboam does not repent (1 Kings 13:33: even after this he did not turn from his evil ways).
7: Jeroboam offers hospitality: “Come home with me and refresh yourself (hittaḥzaq, strengthen yourself), and I will give you a gift.” hittaḥzaq (from ḥāzaq, H2388, to be strong) in the context of hospitality means to receive refreshment/sustenance. The offer is a political move — the king who has just experienced the prophet’s authority now offers to domesticate him through hospitality and reward. The gift (māttān, H4976) would create patronage-obligation: the prophet who accepts the king’s gift becomes the king’s beneficiary. The man of God’s refusal in 1 Kings 13:8-9 is not rudeness but the preservation of prophetic independence from royal patronage.
8: The man of God refuses: “If you gave me half your palace, I would not go with you, nor eat bread nor drink water in this place.” The absoluteness of the refusal — even hypothetically the largest imaginable royal gift would not change the answer — is the prophetic declaration of independence from patronage. lōʾ-ʾōḵal leḥem wĕlōʾ-ʾešteʾ māyim bammāqôm hazzeh (I will not eat bread nor drink water in this place) — the explicit prohibition from YHWH applies to this place (maqôm), not merely to the king. The place itself is under prophetic judgment; receiving its hospitality would imply fellowship with what is under condemnation.
9: kî-ḵēn ṣiwwānî bĕḏĕḇar YHWH — for so he commanded me by the word of YHWH. The man of God’s threefold prohibition — no bread, no water, no return by the same road — is the specific divine command (dĕḇar YHWH as binding obligation) that governs his mission. The prohibition against eating or drinking in Bethel signals the separateness required of YHWH’s messenger in a defiled cultic environment. The prohibition against returning by the same road is unexplained but may signal that the messenger’s presence has effected a one-directional change — the word has been spoken, cannot be unspoken, and the messenger cannot retrace it. This specific command will be the instrument of his death when he disobeys it (1 Kings 13:19).
10: He went home by a different route and did not return the way he came to Bethel. The obedience is precise: wayyelek bĕdereḵ ʾaḥer (went by another way) — verbal echo of the specific command. The man of God has fulfilled his mission at Bethel with complete fidelity. His vulnerability arises not from lack of obedience in the mission but from susceptibility to deception in the aftermath. The DtrH’s point is not that prophets are generally unreliable but that even the genuinely commissioned messenger can be deceived when he leaves his divine instruction and accepts human testimony over the divine word.
11: An old prophet (nāḇîʾ ʾeḥāḏ zāqēn) was living in Bethel; his sons reported to him what the man of God had done that day in Bethel. The old Bethel prophet is an enigmatic figure — why does a prophet of YHWH live at Jeroboam’s cult center? He may be a resident prophet who has compromised with the existing religious establishment, or he may be genuinely prophetic but operating within a corrupted cultic context. His sons witnessed the entire Bethel event; their report to their father sets up the old prophet’s deceptive pursuit of the man of God.
12: The sons showed their father the road the man of God from Judah had taken. The detail that the sons saw the road and reported it to their father is the narrative preparation for the pursuit. The old prophet’s knowledge of the man of God’s route enables his deception. The narrator does not explain the old prophet’s motivation for pursuing and deceiving the man of God — whether envy, curiosity, or something more sinister. The moral ambiguity of the old Bethel prophet is one of the chapter’s most theologically dense elements.
13: “Saddle the donkey for me” (ḥābĕšû-lî haḥămôr) — the old prophet’s urgency is expressed in the command-form. The donkey as transport reflects the same economic world as 1 Sam 9 — the significant animal whose saddling marks a journey with purpose. The sons saddle it and the old prophet rides after the man of God. The deliberate pursuit of the man of God by the old prophet — on a donkey, covering the same road in reverse — is the setup for the decisive deception of 1 Kings 13:18.
14: He found him sitting under an oak tree (yôšēḇ taḥat hāʾallôn). The oak tree as resting spot after the prophetic mission — the man of God sitting in the shade after his task — is a moment of vulnerability. The sitting man who has completed his work is more susceptible to social interaction than the moving man with mission-focus. The old prophet’s first question — “Are you the man of God who came from Judah?” — establishes identification before the deceptive invitation of 1 Kings 13:15.
15: “Come home with me and eat bread.” The invitation is the same as Jeroboam’s (1 Kings 13:7) but without the gift-component — it is presented as simple hospitality rather than patronage. The repetition of the identical invitation (with slight variation) that the man of God has already refused once is the test of his faithfulness to the divine command. That he succumbs to the old prophet’s version when he resisted the king’s reveals that the social authority of a fellow prophet is more persuasive than royal patronage in breaking down his resolve.
16: The man of God’s response is identical to his response to Jeroboam (1 Kings 13:8-9): “I cannot go back with you or go in with you; I will not eat bread or drink water with you in this place.” His initial response to the old prophet is correct — the dĕḇar YHWH binding him is cited again (lōʾ-ʾûḵal lāšûḇ ʾittĕḵā). His correct refusal in 1 Kings 13:16 sets up the tragedy of his capitulation in 1 Kings 13:19 — demonstrating that the failure was not a simple moral lapse but a doctrinal error: he accepted a claimed prophetic word over the actual word already received from YHWH.
17: dāḇār YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr lōʾ-tōʾkal leḥem wĕlōʾ-ṯišteʾ-māyim šām — a word of YHWH was spoken to me saying: Do not eat bread or drink water there. The man of God states the specific divine command clearly in refusing the old prophet’s invitation. The clarity of his knowledge of the command — he can articulate it precisely — makes his subsequent failure to abide by it more tragic. He is not confused about the command; he is convinced by a false word that the command has been modified. The epistemological problem is the addition of a second (false) prophetic word to the first (true) one.
18: gam-ʾănî nāḇîʾ kāmôḵā ûmalʾāḵ dibbēr ʾēlay bĕḏĕḇar YHWH lēʾmōr hăšibēhû ʾittĕḵā ʾel-bêteḵā wĕyōʾkal leḥem wĕyišteʾ-māyim kāzēḇ lô — I too am a prophet like you, and an angel spoke to me by the word of YHWH saying: Bring him back with you to your house so he may eat bread and drink water. He lied to him. The narrator’s insertion — kāzēḇ lô (he lied to him) — is one of the most direct authorial intrusions in the Hebrew Bible. The claim to prophetic authority plus angelic mediation was designed to override the man of God’s direct divine command. The word kāzēḇ (H3577, a lie) leaves no ambiguity: the angel story is fabricated. The DtrH’s point is that a claimed prophetic word that contradicts a received divine command must be rejected, regardless of the authority claimed (cf. Galatians 1:8: “if we or an angel from heaven should preach a gospel contrary to the one we preached...”).
19: The man of God went back with him and ate and drank in his house. The capitulation to the false prophetic word is described with stark simplicity. The man of God has violated his specific divine command on the basis of a claimed competing prophetic authority. The DtrH’s theological point is precise: the word of YHWH already received is normative; a subsequent word that contradicts it, however it is packaged (even as angelic mediation), is not to be believed (cf. Deuteronomy 13:1-5). The man of God’s failure is not a moral failure but a theological one — he privileges social authority over the word already given.
20: While they were sitting at the table, the dĕḇar YHWH came to the prophet who had brought him back. The divine word now comes through the very prophet who lied — the same channel of the false word is now the channel of true judgment. YHWH’s use of the false prophet as the instrument of true pronouncement against the man of God he deceived is a severe irony: the lie becomes the occasion for the truth it attempted to suppress. The authenticity of this word (unlike 1 Kings 13:18) is confirmed by its fulfillment (1 Kings 13:24-26).
21: yaʿan kî mārîṯā pî YHWH — because you disobeyed the mouth of YHWH. The charge is direct: māraʾ (H4784, to be rebellious/disobedient) against pî YHWH (the mouth of YHWH — YHWH’s direct spoken word). The man of God’s sin is defined precisely: not general moral failure but specific disobedience to a specific divine command. The category māraʾ is used for Israel’s rebellion in the wilderness (Numbers 20:24; 1 Kings 27:14) — the same vocabulary connects the individual prophet’s failure to the national pattern.
22: Because he returned and ate bread and drank water in the place where YHWH had told him not to, his body would not come to the tomb of his fathers (lōʾ-yāḇôʾ nĕḇilātĕḵā ʾel-qeḇer ʾăḇōṯêḵā). The specific punishment — denial of burial in the ancestral tomb — is a severe covenant curse. In Israelite theology, burial with one’s fathers was the completion of the covenant inheritance; to be denied it was to be cut off from the community’s covenantal continuity. nĕḇēlāh (H5038, corpse) is the term for an unburied or dishonored body — the man of God dies and his body lies on the road, consumed by a lion, which is the opposite of the honored burial he expected.
23: After the man of God had eaten and drunk, the old prophet saddled the donkey for him. The hospitality that was the vehicle of the deception is now completed with full courtesy — the old prophet saddling the donkey for his guest’s departure. The ordinary social courtesies continue even after the word of judgment has been pronounced. The man of God departs not knowing that the word of judgment he just heard from the prophet who deceived him will be immediately executed on the road.
24: A lion met him on the road and killed him. The lion stands beside the body; the donkey also stands beside it. The lion that kills the man of God is YHWH’s instrument of covenant discipline — the lion as covenant executor appears also in 2 Kings 17:25-26 (lions sent by YHWH against the non-Israelites who resettled Samaria) and in the covenant curse list (Leviticus 26:22: wild animals that devour). The lion does not eat the body nor harm the donkey — its task is execution, not consumption. The restraint of the lion is the supernatural evidence that this death is judicial, not accidental predation.
25: Men passing by saw the body on the road with the lion standing beside it and reported it in the city where the old prophet lived. The public visibility of the scene — the lion standing beside the body without eating it — is the self-authenticating nature of the divine judgment. The news reaches the old prophet who had been the proximate cause of the man of God’s return. The passers-by’s report begins the chain of events that will bring the man of God to burial — ironically through the very prophet who had caused his death.
26: ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm hûʾ ʾăšer māraʾ ʾeṯ-pî YHWH wayyittĕnēhû YHWH lāʾărî — It is the man of God who disobeyed the mouth of YHWH; YHWH has given him to the lion. The old prophet’s recognition-speech is simultaneously his testimony against himself — the prophet who lied and caused the man of God’s disobedience now identifies the judicial execution as YHWH’s action. The formula wayyittĕnēhû YHWH lāʾărî (YHWH gave him to the lion) uses the divine-agency formula for judicial execution.
27: The old prophet saddles the donkey a second time — the same action as in 1 Kings 13:13, forming the chapter’s narrative bracket. The first saddling initiated the deception; the second initiates the act of respect for the deceased. The chapter’s moral complexity is concentrated in the old prophet: deceiver, recipient of true prophetic word, and ultimately the one who buries and mourns the man he destroyed.
28: He found the body on the road — the lion and donkey still standing beside it, the lion had not eaten the body nor harmed the donkey. The restated observation of the lion’s restraint (cf. 1 Kings 13:24) is the double-witness to the supernatural character of the execution. The body lying honorably on the road — uneaten despite a lion standing guard — is a visually paradoxical image of both judgment and dignity. The DtrH preserves the detail because the lion’s restraint authenticates the divine agency.
29: The old prophet picked up the man of God’s body, laid it on the donkey, and brought it back to the city to mourn and bury him. The act of retrieval and proper burial — despite the judgment that the man of God’s body would not reach his fathers’ tomb — is the old prophet’s act of covenant honor for the deceased. The mourning (yisspōḏ, H5594, to beat the breast/lament) is genuine; the burial in the old prophet’s own tomb is an act of covenant solidarity with the one he had caused to die.
30: wayyisspōḏ ʾālāyw ʾôy ʾāḥî — he mourned over him: “Alas, my brother!” The lament formula hôy ʾāḥî (woe/alas, my brother) is the covenant mourning cry (cf. Jeremiah 22:18 where the same formula is denied to the wicked king — the denial of the mourning-cry is itself a curse). The old prophet’s “my brother” (ʾāḥî) acknowledges the prophetic brotherhood between them — two men of God, one deceived by the other. The mourning is genuine even as the old prophet bears responsibility for the death.
31: wayĕhî ʾaḥărê qobrô ʾōṯô wayyōʾmer ʾel-bānāyw — after he buried him, he said to his sons: “When I die, bury me in the same grave as the man of God.” The old prophet’s final wish — burial alongside the man he deceived — is the expression of his confidence in the man of God’s oracle. By choosing burial in the same tomb, he is placing himself under the protection of the man of God’s prophetic authority — specifically the Josiah prophecy of 1 Kings 13:2. The tomb at Bethel will be spared when Josiah desecrates the other tombs (2 Kings 23:17-18: Josiah saw the marker of the man of God’s tomb and preserved it). The old prophet’s deathbed wish is both an act of penitence and a prophetic foresight.
32: kî hāyôʾ yihyeh haddāḇār ʾăšer qārāʾ bĕḏĕḇar YHWH ʾal-hammizḇēaḥ — for the word he proclaimed by the word of YHWH against the altar in Bethel will surely come to pass. The old prophet’s posthumous testimony to the reliability of the man of God’s oracle is stated in the emphatic infinitive absolute construction (hāyôʾ yihyeh) — “it will surely, certainly come to pass.” The prophet who lied about angelic messages now attests truly to the prophetic word he had sought to suppress. His final testimony validates the oracle he had attempted to circumvent.
33: wĕʾaḥar haddāḇār hazzeh lōʾ-šāb yārāḇʿām middarkô hārāʿāh — even after this, Jeroboam did not turn from his evil way. The lōʾ-šāb (did not turn) is the DtrH’s repentance formula in reverse: the covenant call to šûḇ (return/repent, H7725) was not heeded. “After this” (ʾaḥar haddāḇār hazzeh) refers to the signs, the withered hand, and the entire 1 Kings 13 episode — Jeroboam witnessed prophetic authentication of YHWH’s word and still continued appointing non-Levitical priests for the high places. The hardened king is the DtrH’s object lesson: miraculous signs do not produce repentance in those who have decided against the covenant.
34: wayhî baddāḇār hazzeh lĕḥaṭṭāʾṯ bêṯ-yārāḇʿām — this became the sin of the house of Jeroboam, to cut it off and to wipe it from the face of the earth. The summary formula closes the Jeroboam section with the DtrH’s verdict: the ḥēṭ (sin/transgression) is not merely personal but dynastic — it will “cut off” (lĕhakrît, H3772, to cut off/exterminate, the covenant curse term of Leviticus 26:30) the entire Jeroboam dynasty. mĕḥôṯ ûlĕʾabbēḏ (to destroy and to cause to perish) — two verbs of complete elimination. The founding apostasy of the northern kingdom carries within it the sentence of the entire dynasty’s destruction.
